summary: Gruber recounts cracking his iPhone's glass back for the second consecutive year, arguing that glass is a fundamentally poor material for phone backs despite its necessity for inductive charging and wireless signals. He ran an identical poll on both Mastodon and Twitter asking followers about case usage, finding that nearly a third go caseless, a third use Apple cases, and a third use third-party cases. Surprisingly, Mastodon generated more responses than Twitter despite having a fraction of the followers. The post transitions into detailed reviews of his preferred iPhone cases, all sharing one key trait: no bottom lip, which he considers essential for comfortable swipe-up gestures.
key_points: Glass is a terrible material for phone backs — fragile and crack-prone — but currently irreplaceable due to inductive charging and wireless signal requirements
Gruber's poll of 17,000+ responses showed roughly equal thirds for no case, Apple case, and third-party case, with caseless usage much higher than the general population's ~20%
Mastodon outperformed Twitter in engagement (9,171 vs 7,880 responses) despite Gruber having nearly 10x fewer followers there
Apple changed their cases to include a full bottom lip, which Gruber considers a regression for the swipe-up gesture experience
Gruber reviews several premium cases (Bullstrap, Pitaka, Anson Calder) that share his key requirement: no bottom lip and MagSafe support
money_quotes: Glass, all things considered, is a rather terrible material for the back of a phone.
An expensive product you carry around in your pocket or purse ought to be made of sturdy materials, not fragile ones. Plastic would work, but plastic isn't a luxury material.
At the moment, only unobtainium seemingly fits the bill.
Apple has never designed iPhones thinking they need to be in a case.
Again, by no means am I even vaguely trying to argue that Mastodon is a more popular platform with more engagement than Twitter overall. But amongst my followers, it clearly is.
AppleCare+ is my go-to iPhone case.

I'm a gambler: I haven't purchased AppleCare for any product since my Macintosh LC for college in 1991, and through some combination of good fortune and careful handling, I'm now way ahead for having forgone it so many times.
If I could make one request of Pitaka, it would be for a case that is all black, instead of striped black-and-gray.
key_insight: The iPhone case debate reveals a fundamental design tension: Apple builds phones meant to be used naked, yet uses a material so fragile that a significant majority of users feel compelled to cover them up.
